content.sfdetails__c:
Chapter 11: Update or upgrade BIG-IP VIPRION systems (vCMP)

Table of contents | << Previous chapter | Next chapter >>

Contents

Chapter sections

  • Introduction
    • The VIPRION system and vCMP module
    • vCMP update or upgrade considerations
    • Upgrade vs. update
  • Prerequisites
  • Update or upgrade the first vCMP host and standby vCMP guest systems
    • Prepare to update or upgrade the vCMP host
    • Update or upgrade the vCMP host
    • Prepare to update or upgrade the vCMP guest
    • Update or upgrade the vCMP guest
  • Change the updated or upgraded vCMP guest system from standby to active
    • Prepare to fail over the updated or upgraded system
    • Fail over the updated or upgraded system
  • Update or upgrade the other systems
    • Repeat the updating or upgrading process

Introduction

Use this procedure when you want to upgrade:

  • A pair of BIG-IP VIPRION Virtual Clustered Multiprocessing (vCMP) host systems with a new version of BIG-IP software.
  • A vCMP guest high availability (HA) device group that is running on the vCMP host.

The following video demonstrates the entire upgrade.

To watch a video demo of this procedure, go to  Upgrading BIG-IP VIPRION systems.

There are three parts to this upgrade:

  • Upgrading the first vCMP host and standby vCMP guest systems
  • Changing the upgraded vCMP guest system from standby to active
  • Upgrading the other systems

The VIPRION system and vCMP module

The VIPRION system supports multiple blades that work together as a cluster to process application traffic. A VIPRION cluster is the group of active blades in the VIPRION chassis.

The VIPRION system can run the vCMP module, which you can provision on VIPRION and other platforms. vCMP allows you to run multiple instances of BIG-IP software on the same platform.

A vCMP host is the system-wide hypervisor that makes it possible for you to create and view BIG-IP instances, known as guests.

A vCMP guest is an instance of BIG-IP software that you create on the vCMP system. The guest enables you to provision one or more BIG-IP modules to process application traffic.

vCMP update and upgrade considerations

Refer to the below list for vCMP upgrade considerations:

  • For high availability (HA) configurations, run the vCMP hosts as standalone systems and the guests in HA device groups.
  • Hosts and guests are upgraded independently of one another.
  • Plan the order for host/guest upgrades according to the following guidance:
    • To determine vCMP host/guest compatibility for your platform, refer to K14088: vCMP host and compatible guest version matrix.
    • If you are upgrading the vCMP system to a version that would temporarily result in the vCMP host and guest running incompatible versions, F5 recommends that you upgrade the guest instance first and then activate the boot location for the newly upgraded guest. Next, on the vCMP host, set the upgraded guest to the Provisioned state. You can then safely upgrade the vCMP host to the new, compatible version. Once you have rebooted the host to the new version, set the vCMP guest to Deployed.
    • For all other cases, F5 recommends that you upgrade the host first.
  • F5 recommends that you configure your vCMP host to run the same BIG-IP version as the latest version used by any of its vCMP guests.
  • Software images that are stored and managed on the vCMP host are available for vCMP guests to install.
  • Only upgrade one vCMP guest, per slot, at a time. You can upgrade guests on separate slots at the same time.
  • Each guest inherits the license of the vCMP host, and the host license includes all BIG-IP modules available for use with vCMP guest instances. If you need to reactivate the license, you reactivate it at the vCMP host only.

Upgrade vs. update

This chapter may use the word upgrade to refer to either a software upgrade or update. For a definition that explains the difference between the two terms, refer to Upgrading vs. updating in the Introduction.

Prerequisites

Complete the following tasks before you start upgrading your VIPRION systems:

  • Verify that the management interface for each slot is physically wired to an external switch, so that you can maintain connectivity to the management port if the primary blade designation changes during the update.
  • Consult K9476: The F5 hardware/software compatibility matrix to ensure you upgrade to a BIG-IP version that is compatible with your VIPRION platform.
  • For vCMP systems, consult K14088: vCMP host and compatible guest version matrix to ensure you upgrade your host and guests to BIG-IP versions that are compatible.
  • Consult K13845: Overview of supported BIG-IP upgrade paths and an upgrade planning reference to identify the BIG-IP versions that you can upgrade to from your current version of BIG-IP.
  • Review the release notes for the version you want to install.
  • F5 recommends that you reactivate the host license before you upgrade. However, you can verify the service check date for the license to see if you need to reactivate it. For more information, refer to K7727: License activation may be required before a software upgrade for the BIG-IP or Enterprise Manager system.
  • Consult K02251382: B2250 VIPRION Fails to boot After Upgrade to 13.1.3.3 or 13.1.3.4 if you have a VIPRION B2250 blade and you are upgrading your host to BIG-IP 13.1.3.3 or 13.1.3.4. Note that this only applies to hosts and not guests.

Update or upgrade the first vCMP host and standby vCMP guest systems

Impact of procedures: Performing the following procedures should not have a negative impact on your system.

Prepare to update or upgrade the vCMP host

Check the software images

  1. Log in to the Configuration utility for the VIPRION system you are upgrading.
  2. Go to System > Software Management.
  3. Under Installed Images, check the version and boot location.
  4. If you previously downloaded the images you want to install on the vCMP host and guest, verify the images are listed under Available Images.

Check the VIPRION clusters

  1. Go to System > Clusters.
  2. At the bottom of the page, verify the status for the slots you are using display as green.

    The green status icon indicates a slot is active and running properly.

Force the guest into Standby status, when necessary

Check the status of the guest

On the host you are planning to upgrade, check the vCMP guest to ensure it is in Standby status before you upgrade. If it is, you can skip the next step. If the guest is active, in the next step, you fail over to the guest on the host you are not upgrading.

  1. Go to vCMP > Guest List.
  2. In the list, identify the guests that are on your host.
  3. Go to vCMP > Guest Status.
  4. Under Prompt Status, check the status of the guest. If it is Standby, you can skip the next step.

Fail over to the guest on the host you are not updating or upgrading

If the guest is currently active, you must fail over to the guest on the host you are not upgrading. Log in to the active guest and force the fail over status for the traffic group to Standby.

  1. Log in to the Configuration utility for the guest on the host you are upgrading.
  2. Go to Device Management > Traffic Groups.
  3. In the list, select the traffic group for this device.
  4. Select Force to Standby, and then select Force to Standby again.

Check the status of the guest you failed over to

Ensure the guest you failed over to is active and passing traffic.

  1. Log in to the Configuration utility for the guest you failed over to.
  2. Go to Device Management > Traffic Groups.
  3. Under Failover Status, ensure the status is Active.
  4. Go to Local Traffic > Virtual Servers.
  5. In the list, ensure that the icon for the virtual server is green, which indicates that it is active.

Validate the host configuration

Ensure the host system does not have issues that will prevent the configuration from loading after you upgrade.

  1. Open the command line for the host you are upgrading.
  2. Enter the following command:

    tmsh load sys config verify

    This command verifies the configuration without making any changes to the running configuration.

  3. Check the system output for any issues.

Create a UCS archive for the host

Create an archive of the host you are upgrading and download it to your local system. If you encounter a problem during the upgrade, you can use it to restore the host configuration.

  1. Open the Configuration utility for the host you are upgrading.
  2. Go to System > Archives > Add (+).
  3. In File name, enter a name for the archive.
  4. Select Finished.
  5. Select OK.
  6. In the Archive List, select the UCS file you just created.
  7. In Archive File, select Download <file name>.
  8. Select OK.

Import and verify the software image

For information about how to import the software image, watch the following video, or refer to the Importing a new software image to the BIG-IP system section in K34745165: Managing software images on the BIG-IP system.

To watch a video demo of this procedure, go to  Importing a software image into the BIG-IP system.

Shut down the guest on the host

You already failed over the guest on the host you are upgrading to the guest on the other VIPRION host. Before you upgrade, shut down the guest on the host you are upgrading.

  1. Go to vCMP > Guest List.
  2. In the row for the guest you want to shut down, select the check box, and then select Configure.
  3. Select OK.

Update or upgrade the vCMP host

Install the update or upgrade

  1. Go to System > Software Management > Image List.
  2. Under Available Images, select the check box for the software image you want to install, and then select Install.
  3. In Volume set name, select an existing volume, or enter a volume.
  4. Select Install.
  5. To monitor the progress of the installation, periodically select the Image List tab to refresh the page. Under Installed Images, in the Install Status column, you can monitor the progress of the installation.

    When the software finishes installing, Install Status displays as complete. You can see the version and boot location, and that the version is not active.

Reboot to the volume that is running the update or upgrade

  1. Go to System > Software Management > Boot Locations.
  2. In the list, select the boot location you want.
  3. Select Activate and then OK.

    The system restarts.

Check the update or upgrade

After the host restarts, ensure the system is running the upgrade and the chassis slots are active and functioning properly.

  1. After the host restarts, log in to the Configuration utility.
  2. Go to Software Management > Image List.
  3. Under Installed Images, in the row for the version you just installed, in the Active column, ensure the upgrade you just installed is the active version.
  4. Go to System > Clusters > Properties.
  5. At the bottom of the page, verify the status for the slots you are using is green.

Prepare to update or upgrade the vCMP guest

The guest on the host may be running a different BIG-IP version and require a different upgrade.

Deploy the guest before you update or upgrade

Before you upgraded the host, you shut down the guest. Now you must restart it by deploying it.

  1. Go to vCMP > Guest List.
  2. In the row for the guest you want, select the check box, and then select Deploy.

    You can watch the deployment progress in the Status column. This process can take a few minutes.

  3. After the guest finishes deploying, in the Management IP Address column, note the IP address.
  4. Go to vCMP > Guest Status.

    In the row for the guest, in the Prompt Status column, you can see that the guest is in Standby status.

Check the current software version on the guest

  1. Open the Configuration utility for the guest you are upgrading.
  2. Go to System > Software Management > Image List.
  3. Under Installed Images, in the Active column, locate the version of BIG-IP that is active on the guest.

Validate the guest configuration

Ensure the guest does not have any issues that will prevent the configuration from loading after you upgrade.

  1. Open the command line for the guest you are upgrading.
  2. Enter the following command:

    tmsh load sys config verify

  3. Check the system output for any issues.

Synchronize the guests, when necessary

When you make changes or changes are pending on a guest, you must synchronize the changes to the other guest before you upgrade.

  1. Open the Configuration utility for the guest you are upgrading.
  2. Go to Device Management > Overview.
  3.  Under Sync Issues, in the box for each pending change, select Sync.

Create a UCS archive for the guest

Create an archive of the guest and download it to your local system. If you encounter a problem during the upgrade, you can use it to restore the guest configuration.

  1. Go to System > Archives > Add (+).
  2. In File name, enter a name for the archive.
  3. Select Finished.
  4. Select OK.
  5. In the list, select the UCS file you just created.
  6. In Archive File, select Download <file name>.
  7. Select OK.

Set the device group to manual sync

If the device group is set to synchronize automatically, before upgrading, change the setting so that it synchronizes manually.

  1. Go to Device Management > Device Groups.
  2. In the list, select the device group name.
  3. Under Configuration, in the Sync Type list, select Manual with Incremental Sync.
  4. Select Update.

Optional: Setting Minimum Blades Up for vCMP guests

To watch a video demo of this procedure, go to  Set Minimum Blades Up for vCMP guests.

As a precautionary measure, to prevent the guest you are upgrading from becoming active during the upgrade or when it goes back online after you upgrade, you can set the Minimum Blades Up setting to a number greater than the total number of blades in the chassis to intentionally trigger a failsafe condition.

Impact of procedure: While the system is in a failsafe condition, it does not accept connections to local traffic listeners, such as virtual servers or secure network address translations (SNATs).

  1. Open the command line and log in to the TMOS Shell (tmsh) on the guest you are upgrading by typing the following command:

    tmsh

  2. Change Minimum Blades Up to a number that is greater than the total number of blades in the chassis.

    For example, in a VIPRION chassis with four blades, set Minimum Blades Up to 5, by entering the following command:

    modify /sys cluster default min-up-members 5

  3. Enable Minimum Blades Up by entering the following command:

    modify /sys cluster default min-up-members-enabled yes

  4. The HA status for the guest changes to Failsafe Fault to indicate that the system does not accept connections.
  5. To revert, perform steps 1 through 3, and then for Minimum Blades Up in step 2, enter a value lower than the number of current available blades.

Update or upgrade the vCMP guest

Install the update or upgrade

  1. Go to System > Software Management.
  2. Under Available Images, in the row for software image you want to install, select the check box, and then select Install.
  3. In the Install Software Image box, in Volume set name, enter the volume you want to use.
  4. Select Install.
  5. To monitor the progress of the installation, periodically select the Image List tab to refresh the page. Under Installed Images, in the Install Status column, you can monitor the progress of the installation.

    When the software finishes installing, Install Status displays as complete. You can see the version and boot location, and that the version is not active.

Reboot the volume that is running the update or upgrade

  1. Go to System > Software Management > Boot Locations.
  2. In the list, select the boot location you want.
  3. Select Activate and then OK.

    The system restarts.

Check the update or upgrade

After the guest restarts, ensure the system is running the upgrade.

  1. After the guest restarts, log in to the Configuration utility.
  2. Go to System > Software Management.
  3. Under Installed Images, in the row for the version you just installed, in the Active column, ensure the upgrade you just installed is the active version.

Change the updated or upgraded vCMP guest system from standby to active

Impact of procedures: Performing the following procedures should not have a negative impact on your system.

Prepare to fail over to the updated or upgraded system

The guest you did not upgrade is active and running the older version of software. Switch the traffic from that guest to the upgraded guest. To do so, on the guest you did not upgrade, force the Failover status for the traffic group to Standby.

Verify that the guest you did not update or upgrade is still passing traffic

  1. Open the Configuration utility for the guest you did not upgrade.
  2. Go to Local Traffic > Virtual Servers.
  3. In the row for each virtual server, in the Status column, ensure the icon is green.

Fail over to the updated or upgraded system

Force the guest you did not update or upgrade to Standby status

  1. Go to Device Management > Traffic Groups.
  2. In the list, select the traffic group for this device.
  3. At the bottom of the page, select Force to Standby, and then select Force to Standby again.

Check the status of the updated or upgraded guest

  1. Log in to the Configuration utility for the upgraded guest.
  2. Go to Device Management > Traffic Groups.
  3. Under Failover Status, ensure the status is Active.
  4. Go to Local Traffic > Virtual Servers > Virtual Server List.
  5. In the row for each virtual server, in the Status column, ensure the icon is green.
  6. To also verify that the virtual server is taking active connections, go to Virtual Servers > Statistics > Virtual Server and in row for each virtual server, in the Bits and Packets columns, check the activity.

Update or upgrade the other systems

Impact of procedures: Performing the following procedures should not have a negative impact on your system.

Repeat the updating or upgrading process

After you finish upgrading the first host and guest, you can repeat the first part of this process–Updating and upgrading the vCMP host and standby vCMP guest systems–on the other host and guest.
